content:
one of Roc's nicest unique features is being able to decode based on return type, e.g. 

user = File.read(path, Json.utf8)?

I consider it absolutely essential that we preserve this capability in the static dispatch world

in the static dispatch world, something we haven't settled on is:

  • what does the type look like for functions where we're dispatching on return type instead of on the first argument? (first argument being the much more common "method style")
  • what does the syntax look like at the expression level for doing this? Like we have method syntax for saying "go look up this type's module and pass it as the first argument" but what if we want to go look up the return type's module? What's the expression-level syntax for that?

here's an idea:

decode_bytes : List U8 -> a
    where *.decode : List U8 -> a
decode_bytes = |bytes| =
    *.decode(bytes)
so if I used this on a User type which is defined in a module named User.roc, then *.decode(bytes) would resolve to User.decode(bytes)

and if you don't have a type annotation, *.foo(bar) would basically say "I'm looking for a module that exposes a foo function which accepts bar as its only argument, and I'm going to find that module by looking up what module the return type of *.foo(bar) is defined in"

in this design, we'd have two ways to express static dispatch constraints:

    where a.decode(List U8) -> Something

...means "the module where a is defined exposes a function named decode which takes an a as its first argument and List U8 as its second argument, and returns a `Something"

    where *.decode : List U8 -> a

...means "the module where a is defined exposes a function named decode which takes a List U8 and returns an a"
one of the things that's annoying about designing this syntax is that it's so valuable for it to exist, yet it comes up so rarely :sweat_smile:
Why do we need *.decode(bytes), why would it just be bytes.decode()? I feel like I'm missing something obvious...
hm, I guess actually it could be :thinking:
although I'm not sure what the body of that function would be
to have its type end up being what we'd need it to be
yeah I think calling bytes.decode() just moves the question to be "what is the type and body of List.decode?" - at which point something like .* becomes necessary

in fact, I think the example I used above would actually be what it would have to look like :big_smile: 

List.decode_bytes : List U8 -> a
    where *.decode : List U8 -> a
List.decode_bytes = |bytes| =
    *.decode(bytes)

if that function were in List, it would make bytes.decode() work - but the function itself would still need to exist

I'm wondering if/how this would work for functions that return compound types. An example from the other thread on this (#ideas > static dispatch - decoding):

where
    module answer as M,
    M.decode(List U8, Str, fmt) -> Result answer DecodingErr

If simplified and applied to the current thread's proposal, this would be:

*.decode(List U8) -> Result answer DecodingErr

This is ambiguous if there are two different variables in the return type, e.g. if the return type was

Result answer other
I think we'd need to at least make the constraint that there is specifically one (not zero, not more than one) type variable in the return type

It seems like it'd be pretty important to have this, but you could definitely get around it by making your custom type something like

StrDecodingResult := [StrDecodingResult (Result Str DecodingErr)]

get_result = |StrDecodingResult.(result)| result
But I'm not sure what the intent was here. Would we want to support compound types or not?

Richard Feldman said:

in this design, we'd have two ways to express static dispatch constraints:

    where a.decode(List U8) -> Something

...means "the module where a is defined exposes a function named decode which takes an a as its first argument and List U8 as its second argument, and returns a `Something"

    where *.decode : List U8 -> a

...means "the module where a is defined exposes a function named decode which takes a List U8 and returns an a"

here's another idea:

    where .(decode) : List U8 -> a
it makes sense and uses real syntax instead of using *. for the first time

So for that type, this would be a body that would infer that:

decode_bytes = |bytes|
    .(decode)(bytes)
Seems maybe ambiguous with anonymous method chains?
hrm yeah I forgot about the body syntax :sweat_smile:
*.decode(bytes) no longer has any other meaning
So I don't think it's a bad thing
It looks weird, but it is weird
This is one of those times when weirdness is the right signal

here's another weird idea:

decode_bytes : List U8 -> a
    where .(decode) : List U8 -> a
decode_bytes = |bytes|
    import _ as Mod

    Mod.decode(bytes)
it's more verbose but I think it communicates better what's happening
The import is good
that is, we're inferring which module the function lives in based on usage
and it's probably fine for it to be verbose given how super rarely it would be used
It's more obvious what's happening here in my eyes
What would you call this? Anonymous module functions?
Trying to name the relevant info for canonicalization

What about 

decode_bytes : List U8 -> a
    where _.decode : List U8 -> a

I think that was suggested before in #ideas > static dispatch - decoding but we wanted a way to refer to the same module doing something. I guess the use of a makes that work, since whatever module _ refers to must contain a
yeah my concern with that is that when I see _.decode it looks like the _ is an expression to me
or a pattern
nothing about it suggests it would be a module
granted, .(decode) doesn't suggest a module either, but it doesn't give a misimpression

decode_bytes : List U8 -> a
    where Module(a) has [a.decode : List U8 -> a]

we suggested this in that thread;

decode_bytes : List U8 -> a
    where a.{ decode : List U8 -> a }
i think Module(a) is very good and unambiguous
It's parens-delimited in a type, which we're currently leaning away from. We should do Module a
sure
or module a, since module can be a lowercase keyword like all of our other keywords

im worried that this is going to lead to some gnarly type annotations though. for example

disk_cached : key, (() -> value) -> value
    where key.encode : key -> List U8 # or whatever syntax
       value.encode : value -> List U8
        Module value .decode : List U8 -> value # or whatever syntax

is to me much harder to parse than

disk_cached : key, (() -> value) -> value
    where key : Encode, value: Encode + Decode
I think you need the a in the specification of the module being referenced though. if you don't include it in _ or * then it's ambiguous when there are two type parameters
Or you require there to be a single type param, no more, no less
how do you write a function that decodes a record of two heterogenous types then
You wouldn't
Not a nice limitation
Not sure how bad that would be, since this is such a niche
Though it would be nice to allow it
i think it's pretty significant. for example the current auto-derived implementations for decoders rely on that

I have no idea what I'm really doing, just riffing of ideas... 

disk_cached : key, (() -> value) -> value
    where
        Encoder key,
        Encoder value,
        Decoder value,

Encoder a : {
    encode : a -> List U8
}

Decoder a : {
    decode : List U8 -> a
}
Conceptually I'm thinking of a Module as if it's just a record with top-level defs as fields
Very Zig

disk_cached : key, (() -> value) -> value
    where
        Encoder key,
        Encoder value,
        Decoder value,

Encoder a : module [
    encode : a -> List U8,
]

Decoder a : module [
    decode : List U8 -> a,
]

# the `module [ .. ]` here mirrors the module export syntax

(edit) switched back to use a type var in the "interface" things
I kinda love it

Or syntax for something that might get inferred

disk_cached : key, (() -> value) -> value
    where
        key : module [
            encode : key -> List U8,
        ],
        value : module [
            encode : key -> List U8,
            decode : List U8 -> a,
        ],

Maybe it would help to also use := if that's how the Custom Types are defined...

disk_cached : key, (() -> value) -> value
    where
        key := module [
            encode : key -> List U8,
        ],
        value := module [
            encode : key -> List U8,
            decode : List U8 -> a,
        ],
This thread has made my brain melt several time but I like this last one Luke the most
It feels like the most natural fit with the rest of the syntax
Verbose but appropriately so for the rareness of it
It screams “pull me out to a top level alias “
But have we reimplemented Abilities as Go-style interfaces?
both Abilities and static dispatch are forms of ad-hoc polymorphism
this is really just a syntax question
Yeah of course

Encoder a : module [
    encode : a -> List U8,
]

Decoder a : module [
    decode : List U8 -> a,
]

Reads to me like

type Encoder interface{
    (Encoder) func encode() []u8
}
Is all I’m saying
But I’ll show my Grug-brained self out :rolling_on_the_floor_laughing:
And I’m pretty sure I have a syntax error in there. A year in Go wasted after 1 month of Rust and Roc
Don't worry about it, Go wasted itself
zing!
Hey I still love Go! Until Roc figures out concurrency that is
Go has quite a few design ideas worth stealing imo
I haven't really used it, so I don't have a complete picture
but things like zero-config formatter, the package version resolution algorithm, etc. seem good
It’s a beautiful but boring language really
It’s my favorite C/Algol descendent in mainstream use
Don’t tell TC-39
Brendan told me that was a Scheme ;)
In his dreams. It’s Self in Javas clothing
He wanted a Scheme, and if he got his way there’d be a lot more Lisp programmers out there (or a lot less web apps)

Just messing with this some more... trying to wrap my head around the problem and simplify it for myself. I think this syntax below works.

I think if we changed module headers to use {} instead of [] for exposing idents, it would mean we could use {} here for the "module interface alias" and that would just be a normal looking type then.

I'm thinking of it as just with a := Mod(a) prefix which tells you this is an alias for a module that has these particular functions exposed for a custom type a, and you can have multiple type vars just like a normal type alias.

Simple examples

# Bool.roc
Eq a := Mod(a) [
    equals : a, a -> Bool,
]

list_equals : List a, List a -> Bool were Mod(a) [ equals: a, a -> Bool ]
list_equals = |first, second| ...

# `Eq` is also a builtin and in scope
list_equals : List a, List a -> Bool were Eq a
list_equals = |first, second| ...

decode_bytes : List U8 -> a where Mod(a) [ decode : List U8 -> a ]
decode_bytes = |bytes| ...

Complicated examples

disk_cached : key, (() -> value) -> value
    where
        Mod(key) [
            encode : key -> List U8,
        ],
        Mod(value) [
            encode : value -> List U8,
            decode : List U8 -> value,
        ],
disk_cached = |k, f| ...

# or if these are defined and in scope
Encoder a := Mod(a) [
    encode : a -> List U8
]

Decoder a := Mod(a) [
    decode : List U8 -> a
]

disk_cached : key, (() -> value) -> value where Encoder key, Encoder value, Decoder value
disk_cached = |k, f| ...

Thinger a b :=
    Mod(a) [
        foo : List a, a -> b,
    ],
    Mod(b) [
        bar : List b, List a -> U64,
    ],

do_big_things : List a, List b -> U64 where Thinger a b

Just threw Thinger a b in there... I'm not sure something like that would be wanted. I figure it would allow for constraining types from across multiple modules and the relationship between them. Maybe that would be a crazy thing to do :shrug:

here's an interesting one:

decode_bytes : List U8 -> a
    where module(a).decode : List U8 -> a
decode_bytes = |bytes|
    import _ as Mod

    Mod.decode(bytes)
so module(a) in the type annotation can be read as "go look up the module where a is defined and put it here"
I really like that module is a lowercase keyword, no clash with tag names like Mod would be

I guess similarly we could use that in expressions:

decode_bytes : List U8 -> a
    where module(a).decode : List U8 -> a
decode_bytes = |bytes|
    module.decode(bytes)

so you could read module.foo(arg) as meaning "infer what module this foo function lives in based on the type we expect foo to return"
the import one is more verbose, but more obvious what it's doing...however, I think there might be a problem with that one, which is that the expression doesn't show which type we're dispatching on

that is, in Mod.decode(bytes) there are at least two types involved in that function call: the argument type and the return type, and since this looks like a normal function call (ModuleName.fn_name(arg)) it's not clear from looking at it that we're dispatching on the return type
and having a different syntax might help with that
because you're like "I don't know what module.foo(arg) means, let me go look that up" and then you see the explanation that it's dispatching on the return type specifically
(and the compiler does need to unambiguously know which one type you want to dispatch on!)

So if we saw

decode_bytes = |bytes, format|
    module.decode(bytes, format)

presumably we'd infer the type

decode_bytes : a, b
    where module(c) : decode(a, b) -> c
And we'd be okay with adding arity to the module(c) thing

If you wanted to put a type of

decode_bytes : a, b
    where module(c, d) : decode(a, b) -> Result c d
Would there be a type problem with that?
I guess module(X) can only take one argument because it refers to the module's custom type
So that wouldn't be a problem
yeah exactly
we could give a nice error message if someone tried to specify more than one type
like "we have to look up the one module where this function exists, so we need to know which one type to use to find that module!"
Also, minor syntax thing: unless we pivot to PNC types, it should probably be module a : decode(List U8) -> a
For consistency
Currently, parens in types are only for methods and grouping
the thing I like about where module(a).decode : is that it feels like the module(a) is directly replacing a fully qualified uppercase module name in front of the .

Richard Feldman said:

decode_bytes : List U8 -> a
    where module(a).decode : List U8 -> a
decode_bytes = |bytes|
    import _ as Mod

    Mod.decode(bytes)

This reads really well.

yeah although I don't think the import part works, because of :

Richard Feldman said:

the expression doesn't show which type we're dispatching on
that is, in Mod.decode(bytes) there are at least two types involved in that function call: the argument type and the return type, and since this looks like a normal function call (ModuleName.fn_name(arg)) it's not clear from looking at it that we're dispatching on the return type
From a type perspective it works, right? Works essentially the same as Decode.decode today.
Decode.decode gets that information from the type declaration in the Ability

but since these are structural, we don't require an up-front declaration which specifies what type we're dispatching on, and therefore need a way to unambiguously specify at the expression level which type you want that function call to dispatch on
I don't fully follow. If we guarantee it is always dispatching on the entire return type for this class of function, isn't that enough?
I guess that is a pretty big restriction

I suspect we may actually need dispatching on the return type to actually be "dispatching on a named type variable" like so:

decode_bytes : List U8 -> Result a DecodingFailed
    where module(a).decode : List U8 -> Result a DecodingFailed
decode_bytes = |bytes|
    module(a).decode(bytes)
so module(a) in both the type and the expression refers to "whatever module the a type is defined in"
the reason I think we need this is that in the case of decoding specifically, it's not enough to say "dispatch on the return type" because the return type is Result :sweat_smile:
so if we dispatch on the return type, we'll go look for the decoding implementation of your custom type in Result.roc, which of course won't be where it is
so I think in this particular case, there's no way to avoid specifying explicitly what type variable you want to go look up, which requires naming it
and then referencing that name in the expression

separately, I realized another problem witeh the import _ as Mod design wouldn't work, which is that if you call two different functions in Mod, then that creates a constraint between them (like, these two functions need to come from the same module, whatever module that turns out to be) which isn't something the type system was planned to be able to express, and which doesn't sound like a useful thing to have
a thing I do like about that code snippet is that you learn what module(a) means once, and then in both places it appears it means the same thing
namely, "whatever module a is defined in"

This would now be the only place in Roc where a type is mentioned in a value, even without type annotations, which is a big barrier to cross. That said, most of my weirdness budget checks on syntax don't apply to this niche of a feature.
I think you'd need a type annotation to use the feature
on the one hand, I'm of course very opposed to requiring type annotations anywhere
but on the other hand, literally what you're saying is "I want to run a different function determined by a particular part of a type"
and I'm not sure it's really possible to say that without saying what the actual type is :sweat_smile:

https://roc.zulipchat.com/#narrow/channel/304641-ideas/topic/static.20dispatch.20-.20dispatch.20on.20return.20types/near/494080982

My idea is more verbose... but it doesnt have this problem if I understand it right.
That message at least mainly talks about type annotations, what would the function body look like that would infer those types?
I think they'd have the same issue Richard mentioned where we can't handle something that returns Result a DecodingErr, only things that return a

This I guess... 

decode_bytes : List U8 -> Result a DecodingFailed
    where Mod(a) [
        decode : List U8 -> Result a DecodingFailed
    ]
decode_bytes = |bytes|
    Mod(_).decode(bytes)

But I suspect it runs in to the same issue with this?

another problem witeh the import _ as Mod design wouldn't work, which is that if you call two different functions in Mod, then that creates a constraint between them

I think essentially requiring a type annotation or a code block that basically specifies the type is fine. That is essentially how abilities work today. This feature feels like it gets a lot simpler with more explicit typing.

yeah when the feature in question is literally "I want this expression to work differently based on a type I want to specify" then it does feel reasonable to require that you actually specify the type :big_smile:

just to make it clear where this stands right now, I think it's fine if we go ahead with this syntax:

Richard Feldman said:

I suspect we may actually need dispatching on the return type to actually be "dispatching on a named type variable" like so:

decode_bytes : List U8 -> Result a DecodingFailed
    where module(a).decode : List U8 -> Result a DecodingFailed
decode_bytes = |bytes|
    module(a).decode(bytes)

it's possible we come up with something better, but the other designs we talked about had some nonobvious problems in terms of under-specifying the relationships between these things, and I think this addresses all of those problems in a way that feels reasonable
What is a just an arbitrary type variable that shows up in code?
And then module becomes a keyword?

could instead write only this with no types:

decode_bytes = |bytes|
    module(decodable).decode(bytes)

Also just to check, is this valid (even if dumb to write):

decode_bytes = |bytes|
    module(bytes).decode(bytes)
I'm not sure if it's viable to write this without types
because it's essentially saying "I want to dispatch on a type that appears somewhere in this function, but I'm not going to tell you which one"
(method-calling syntax tells you which one because it's always the type of the first argument)
so we can't really figure out which one you want from type inference - it would be more like type guessing :sweat_smile:

we could hardcode it to be the return value, but then decoding doesn't work because that doesn't return the type you want to dispatch on, it returns a Result - and the type you want to dispatch on is Result's first type parameter
yeah, got it
Forgot about that
with module(a) where a is a type variable that's in scope, it's clear which one you want
Also, I guess that means all module base dispatch must tie to a method result. You can't transform the data before returning it.
hm, I don't follow :big_smile:

decode_bytes_and_transform : List U8 -> Result b DecodingFailed
    where module(a).decode : List U8 -> Result b DecodingFailed
decode_bytes_and_transform = |bytes|
    module(a).decode(bytes)?
    |> transform_a_to_b
You need to pull out a helper function to generate the a type.
oh, yeah I think where module(a).decode : List U8 -> Result b DecodingFailed would give an error saying a has to appear in the function type
Yeah, just a loss of power compared to Abilities

Cause with abilities, I could at least do:

decode_bytes_and_transform : List U8 -> Result b DecodingFailed
    where module(a).decode : List U8 -> Result b DecodingFailed
decode_bytes_and_transform = |bytes|
    x : a
    x = module(a).decode(bytes)?
    transform_a_to_b(x)
Though in practice with abilities, it would just infer the type from the following use
Abilities explicitly have the "type variable in question must appear in the function" rule :big_smile:
that's how I knew we'd need the restriction!
I don't follow
If I had Decode.fromBytes bytes |> usesSpecificType, it would just work
Cause usesSpecificType would give it enough type info
That was the common case for me

the problem isn't the call site, it's the type:

    where module(a).decode : List U8 -> Result b DecodingFailed

this wouldn't work in Abilities - you couldn't define this as an ability function because both type vars need to be a. This gives a compiler error today.
I don't follow. We have decode working today
List U8, fmt -> DecodeResult val where fmt implements DecoderFormatting

so for example: https://github.com/roc-lang/roc/blob/040453bb0c88871998e49587c1b00fa7691c214f/crates/compiler/builtins/roc/Decode.roc#L80-L81

DecoderFormatting implements
    u8 : Decoder U8 fmt where fmt implements DecoderFormatting
this is an ability function declaration where we have one type variable, fmt, and we're saying "that is the type we're using to resolve the ability" by saying where fmt implements DecoderFormatting"
the syntax is different, but that is conveying the same information as where module(a).decode : List U8 -> Result a DecodingFailed
in the sense that module(a) is how we're saying "the a in Result a DecodingFailed is the type we're dispatching on"

in Ability declarations, DecoderFormatting implements followed by
a function that has where fmt implements DecoderFormatting is how we say "fmt is the type we're dispatching on"

if you take out the where fmt implements DecoderFormatting, you get a compiler error that says basically "this is an ability function for DecoderFormatting, so it has to have a where sometypevariable implements DecoderFormatting"
I'm not sure if I'm explaining this well, but the point is that both syntaxes have the restriction of "you must spell out unambiguously in the type signature which thing is being dispatched on"
module(a) does that by saying that a is the type variable being dispatched on
and also, having where a implements DecoderFormatting - when you're specifically inside the DecoderFormatting ability definition - does that too
module(a) makes it a lot clearer that the restriction exists though I think :big_smile:
Ah, I understand
Restriction at declaration vs restriction at use in this case
But if course you could still make a decode module that wraps the use and hides the restriction some even in the new static dispatch world
totally!

and the reason for needing the syntactic link (to the a type variable) at the call site in the module(a) design is that in the Abilities design, you already have a link between call site and type declaration because the type declaration has to be a named function which has already been formally associated with the ability, so just by calling the function with that name you're indicating that you want to dispatch according to the rules specified by its type
but we don't have that link when it's just a type variable
so module(a) at the call site connects them
Luke Boswell has marked this topic as resolved.

@Anthony Bullard I'm closing this thread and linking the current plan here for reference. If we revisit this design I think we should start a new thread.

#ideas > static dispatch - dispatch on return types @ 💬
